questions.question:
Consider the following statements S1 and S2 about the relational data model:
S1: A relation scheme can have at most one foreign key.
S2: A foreign key in a relation scheme R cannot be used to refer to tuples of R.
Which one of the following choices is correct?
A. Both S1 and S2 are true
B. S1 is true and S2 is false
C. S1 is false and S2 is true
D. Both S1 and S2 are false
questions.answer: D. Both S1 and S2 are false
questions.to_find: Which of the two statements about foreign keys in the relational data model is/are true

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of foreign key constraints in relational databases. Statement S1 claims a relation can have at most one foreign key. Statement S2 claims a foreign key in relation R cannot reference R itself (i.e., no self-referencing). Both claims need to be evaluated against the relational model definition.

keywords.explain: An attribute or set of attributes in a relation R that references the primary key of another relation S (or R itself in a self-reference). It enforces referential integrity.
keywords.example: In EMPLOYEE(empid, name, managerid), managerid is a foreign key referencing empid in the same EMPLOYEE table — a self-referencing foreign key.

keywords.explain: A foreign key in a relation R that references the primary key of the same relation R, used to model hierarchical or recursive relationships.
keywords.example: EMPLOYEE(empid, name, mgr_id) where mgr_id references empid in EMPLOYEE itself.

keywords.explain: The constraint that every foreign key value must either match a primary key value in the referenced relation or be NULL.
keywords.example: If ORDERS(order_id, customer_id) has customer_id as a foreign key referencing CUSTOMERS(customer_id), every customer_id in ORDERS must exist in CUSTOMERS.

understand.visual_alt: Two statements about foreign key constraints evaluated against the relational model: S1 about count restriction, S2 about self-referencing restriction.

given.aim: Determine the truth value of statements S1 and S2 about foreign keys in the relational data model.

terms.meaning: A relation scheme can have at most one foreign key.
terms.example: Claim: REGISTERED_FOR(cno, rollno) cannot have both cno and rollno as separate foreign keys.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — a relation can have multiple foreign keys, each referencing a (possibly different) relation.

terms.meaning: A foreign key in a relation scheme R cannot be used to refer to tuples of R (no self-referencing).
terms.example: Claim: EMPLOYEE(empid, mgr_id) cannot have mgr_id reference empid in EMPLOYEE.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — self-referencing foreign keys are explicitly allowed in the relational model to represent hierarchies.

terms.meaning: The standard database model based on relations (tables) with primary and foreign key constraints.
terms.example: STUDENT(rollno, name) and COURSE(cno, title) with ENROLLED(rollno, cno) where both attributes are foreign keys.
terms.connects: The relational model imposes no restriction on the number of foreign keys, and permits self-referencing.

given.plan: Evaluate S1 against the relational model definition: a relation can have multiple foreign keys (e.g., a many-to-many linking table). Evaluate S2: the relational model explicitly permits self-referencing foreign keys to model recursive relationships. Both statements are false.

steps.title: Evaluate S1: at most one foreign key per relation
steps.apply: Consider a linking table REGISTERED_FOR(cno, rollno). Here, cno is a foreign key referencing COURSES(cno) and rollno is a foreign key referencing STUDENTS(rollno). This single relation has TWO foreign keys. In general, a relation can have as many foreign keys as needed. Therefore S1 is FALSE.
steps.note: There is no restriction in the relational model on the number of foreign keys a relation may have.

steps.title: Evaluate S2: no self-referencing foreign key
steps.apply: Consider EMPLOYEE(empid, name, mgr_id) where mgr_id is a foreign key that references empid in the same EMPLOYEE table. This is a classic self-referencing foreign key used to model managerial hierarchies. The relational model explicitly allows foreign keys to reference the same relation. Therefore S2 is FALSE.
steps.note: Self-referencing (recursive) foreign keys are a standard feature; for example, in category trees and organisational charts.

steps.title: Determine the correct option
steps.apply: S1 is false and S2 is false => option D: Both S1 and S2 are false.
steps.note: Both statements describe non-existent restrictions that the relational model does not impose.

solution.result: D. Both S1 and S2 are false

questions.formula_note: Definition of foreign key constraints in the relational model: multiple foreign keys per relation are allowed, and self-referencing foreign keys are permitted.

questions.question:
Suppose we have a database consisting of the following three relations:
FREQUENTS (CUSTOMER, HOTEL)
SERVES (HOTEL, SNACKS)
LIKES (CUSTOMER, SNACKS)

The first indicates the hotels each customer visits, the second tells which snacks each hotel serves, and the last indicates which snacks are liked by each customer.

Express the following query in relational algebra:
Print the hotels the snacks served by which are liked by customer Rama.
questions.to_find: Relational algebra expression to find hotels all of whose served snacks are liked by customer Rama

understand.plain: We have three tables: FREQUENTS (who visits which hotel), SERVES (which hotel serves which snacks), and LIKES (which customer likes which snacks). We need to find all hotels such that every snack they serve is liked by Rama. This is a 'for all' condition, which maps to the relational division operator.

keywords.explain: An operation R / S that returns all tuples in R that are 'paired with' every tuple in S. Used for universal quantification ('for all') queries.
keywords.example: If SERVES has (H1, S1), (H1, S2), (H2, S1) and the divisor is {S1, S2}, division returns only H1 because H1 serves both S1 and S2.

keywords.explain: Filters rows from a relation based on a condition.
keywords.example: sigma_{CUSTOMER='Rama'}(LIKES) returns only the rows in LIKES where the customer is Rama.

keywords.explain: Extracts specific columns from a relation.
keywords.example: pi_{SNACKS}(sigma_{CUSTOMER='Rama'}(LIKES)) gives the set of snack names liked by Rama.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="520" height="200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="150" height="80" class="bg" rx="6"/><text x="85" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">SERVES</text><text x="85" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">(HOTEL, SNACKS)</text><text x="85" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">H1,S1  H1,S2  H2,S1</text><rect x="180" y="10" width="150" height="80" class="hi" rx="6"/><text x="255" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">LIKES (Rama)</text><text x="255" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">pi_SNACKS(sigma_Rama)</text><text x="255" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">S1, S2</text><text x="350" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">÷</text><rect x="370" y="10" width="130" height="80" class="ok" rx="6"/><text x="435" y="30" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink" style="fill:white">Result</text><text x="435" y="52" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink" style="fill:white">Hotels serving</text><text x="435" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink" style="fill:white">all Rama's snacks</text><text x="260" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">SERVES / pi_{SNACKS}(sigma_{CUSTOMER='Rama'}(LIKES))</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing relational division: SERVES(HOTEL,SNACKS) divided by the set of snacks liked by Rama, yielding hotels that serve all those snacks.

given.aim: Find all hotels such that every snack they serve is liked by customer Rama.

terms.meaning: Records which customer visits which hotel.
terms.connects: Not needed directly for this query since we search over all hotels that serve Rama-liked snacks, regardless of whether Rama visits them.

terms.meaning: Records which snacks a hotel serves.
terms.connects: Dividend in the division: we project out (HOTEL, SNACKS) pairs and divide by Rama's liked snacks.

terms.meaning: The set of all snack names liked by Rama, used as the divisor.
terms.example: If Rama likes Samosa and Vada, this is {Samosa, Vada}.
terms.connects: Divisor in the relational division: a hotel passes only if it serves ALL snacks in this set.

given.plan: Use relational division. The dividend is pi_{HOTEL,SNACKS}(SERVES) and the divisor is pi_{SNACKS}(sigma_{CUSTOMER='Rama'}(LIKES)). The division returns exactly those HOTELs that appear with every snack Rama likes.

steps.title: Identify the query type: universal quantification
steps.apply: The query asks for hotels ALL of whose served snacks are liked by Rama. 'All' quantification in relational algebra maps to the division operator (/).
steps.note: Anytime you see 'for every' or 'all' in a query, think relational division.

steps.title: Compute the divisor: snacks liked by Rama
steps.apply: First select all rows from LIKES where CUSTOMER = 'Rama', then project onto the SNACKS attribute. D contains exactly the set of snacks that Rama likes.
steps.note: This is a single-attribute relation used as the divisor.

steps.title: Form the dividend and apply division
steps.apply: The dividend is pi_{HOTEL,SNACKS}(SERVES) — all (hotel, snack) pairs. Dividing by D gives all HOTEL values h such that for every snack s in D, (h, s) is in SERVES. This is exactly the set of hotels all of whose served snacks are liked by Rama.
steps.note: The result is a unary relation on HOTEL.

questions.formula_note: Relational division (R / S) for universal quantification: finds all tuples in R that are associated with every tuple in S.

questions.question:
Consider the following relational schema:
COURSES (cno, cname)
STUDENTS (rollno, sname, age, year)
REGISTERED_FOR (cno, rollno)

The underlined attributes indicate the primary keys for the relations. The 'year' attribute for the STUDENTS relation indicates the year in which the student is currently studying (First year, Second year etc.).

A. Write a relational algebra query to print the roll number of students who have registered for cno 322.
B. Write a SQL query to print the age and year of the youngest student in each year.

For part A, the relational algebra expression is:
A. pi_{rollno}(sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR))
B. pi_{rollno}(sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR) JOIN STUDENTS)
C. pi_{rollno}(REGISTERED_FOR JOIN sigma_{cno=322}(COURSES))
D. sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR)
questions.to_find: Relational algebra expression to find roll numbers of students registered for course cno=322; and SQL for youngest student age and year per year-group

understand.plain: We have three tables: COURSES (course details), STUDENTS (student details), and REGISTERED_FOR (which student registered for which course). Part A asks for a relational algebra expression to get roll numbers registered for course number 322. Since REGISTERED_FOR already contains both cno and rollno, we only need to filter by cno=322 and project rollno.

keywords.explain: Filters tuples from a relation that satisfy a given predicate.
keywords.example: sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR) keeps only rows where cno equals 322.

keywords.explain: Extracts specified attributes (columns) from a relation, removing duplicates.
keywords.example: pi_{rollno}(R) returns only the rollno column from relation R.

keywords.explain: SQL clause that groups rows sharing a value and applies aggregate functions like MIN() within each group.
keywords.example: SELECT year, MIN(age) FROM STUDENTS GROUP BY year gives the minimum age per year-group.

understand.visual_alt: Schema diagram: COURSES(cno, cname), STUDENTS(rollno, sname, age, year), REGISTERED_FOR(cno, rollno). Query filters REGISTERED_FOR for cno=322 and projects rollno.

given.aim: Part A: Find roll numbers of students registered for cno=322 using relational algebra. Part B: Find age and year of youngest student in each year using SQL.

terms.meaning: A linking table that stores which student (rollno) is registered for which course (cno).
terms.example: (322, 101) means student 101 is registered for course 322.
terms.connects: This relation alone suffices for Part A — no join with STUDENTS or COURSES is needed since it already contains rollno.

terms.meaning: Selection predicate that keeps only tuples where cno equals 322.
terms.example: Filters all registrations to just those for course 322.
terms.connects: Applied to REGISTERED_FOR to isolate the relevant rows.

terms.meaning: Projection onto the rollno attribute, discarding the cno column.
terms.example: From filtered REGISTERED_FOR, extracts only the student roll numbers.
terms.connects: Gives the final answer: a set of distinct roll numbers.

given.plan: Part A: Apply sigma_{cno=322} to REGISTERED_FOR, then pi_{rollno} to get the answer. No join needed. Part B: Use SELECT year, MIN(age) FROM STUDENTS GROUP BY year.

steps.title: Part A: Identify the relevant relation
steps.apply: REGISTERED_FOR(cno, rollno) already contains cno and rollno. To find students registered for cno=322, we do not need to join with COURSES or STUDENTS — the cno and rollno are both present in REGISTERED_FOR.
steps.note: Avoiding unnecessary joins is important for efficiency and correctness in relational algebra.

steps.title: Part A: Apply selection then projection
steps.apply: Step 1: sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR) filters rows to those with cno=322. Step 2: pi_{rollno}(...) projects onto rollno. Result is the set of roll numbers of students registered for course 322.
steps.note: This is the minimal and correct relational algebra expression.

steps.title: Part B: SQL for youngest student age per year-group
steps.formula_raw: SELECT year, MIN(age) FROM STUDENTS GROUP BY year
steps.apply: Group all students by their 'year' attribute (first year, second year, etc.). Within each group, select the minimum age using MIN(age). The query returns one row per year-group showing the year and the age of the youngest student in that year.
steps.note: The 'year' column is both a grouping attribute and a result attribute, so it appears in both SELECT and GROUP BY.

solution.result:
Part A: pi_{rollno}(sigma_{cno=322}(REGISTERED_FOR))
Part B: SELECT year, MIN(age) FROM STUDENTS GROUP BY year;

questions.formula_note: Relational algebra selection-projection pipeline; SQL GROUP BY with MIN aggregate for per-group minimum.
